---
title: My dynamic report
output: html_fragment
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE)

```

```{css}
html,body, div{

  margin: 0 auto;
  background-color: #F5F5F5;
  
}

table{
  margin: 9 auto;
}
img{
  display: block;
  margin: 0 auto;
}

p, h1, h2{
  text-align: center;
}
p{
text-size: 30px;
}

```

## Tracking the Car Color and Model Types in Greenlane.

This Dataset comes from a series of observations where I observerd the population of motor-vehicles along a quieter road in Greenlane. I the recorder was positioned at a Cafe and observed these vehicles to create a database I could apply data visualisation techniques to in this Report.

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(glue)
library(knitr)
# to use a version of f-string in python im also using the glue package, I like the text without "" marks

logged_data <- read.csv("https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/e/2PACX-1vRv5EOizOvtqyfHV7KuizsM1R9lkir6ClXY0T0nzyrCQhfzxgZ0dRq0o5i0BjhWMgZgAX2adOEecKIs/pub?output=csv")


#logged_data
latest_data <- logged_data %>%
  rename(production_year = Year..Numeric., Car_Colour = Color, Maker = Brand, custom_plate = Custom.License.Plate)
#latest_data

#How many observations do we have?
num_observations <- nrow(latest_data)

#cat(glue("We have {num_observations} observations"))

#What is the oldest, and Newest year of cars observed
newest <- max(latest_data$production_year)
oldest <- min(latest_data$production_year)
```

<br>
We have `r num_observations` observations ranging in years from `r oldest` to `r newest`.





```{r}

#paste("We have observations ranging in years from",oldest,"to",newest)

summary_table <- latest_data %>%
  select(
    Color = Car_Colour,
    Year = production_year,
    Manufacturer = Maker,
    
  ) %>%
  as_tibble()
#
summary_table %>% head(10) %>% kable()


#I thought since one of my variables was color I could make the bars match, I did a little research on how to add this functionality
ggplot(data = latest_data) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = Car_Colour, fill = Car_Colour)) + scale_fill_identity() + labs(title = "Comapring car color counts", x = "Car Colour", y = "Total")


# Learnt the arrange function and sorting by descending so I can output a nice summary stat under the data visualisation
# Using Knitr to make my data visualisations nicer, otherwise I have my tables with # and they look less Visually appealing
most_common_colour <- latest_data %>% count(Car_Colour) %>% arrange(desc(n))
most_common_colour %>% kable(col.names = c("colour", "count"))
```

Looking at this plot we see that the most observed colour is `r most_common_colour$Car_Colour[1] ` with `r most_common_colour$n[1] ` observations. 

Notice I have co-ordinated the bar colours to match the color they are measuring, This is one of many new skills I have picked up during this assignment

```{r}


#rotated the text 90 degrees to be able to see the manufacturer names.
ggplot(data = latest_data) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = Maker), fill = "Orange") + theme_minimal() + theme(axis.text.x =  element_text(angle = 90)) 

most_common_brand <- latest_data %>% count(Maker) %>% arrange(desc(n))
most_common_brand %>% kable(col.names = c("Brand", "count"))

```

Looking at this plot we can see clearly that the most common car brand observed is `r most_common_brand$Maker[1] ` with `r most_common_brand$n[1] ` observations,
<br>
 [Heres a link to my form feel free to add an observation fo your own!](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdhYRI6jH7CsPg7w_9M291C8PNFxvXv8mdxJEubVLCk4Rb52w/viewform)



![](https://c.tenor.com/-qBsG1HwR4oAAAAC/tenor.gif)
